APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural conversational modeling—with a methodological contribution (CVAE/kgCVAE) and practical training fixes (KL annealing, bag-of-words loss) that are directly relevant to top-tier venues. It evaluates on standard data (Switchboard, PTB), compares to reasonable baselines (encoder–decoder, VAE-LM), reports hyperparameters, and uses established metrics (BLEU, embedding-based diversity, KL/perplexity), matching community expectations. The presentation (thorough related work, clear stated limitations) and focus on discourse-level diversity target the right audience and standards for a top conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML research topic—neural dialogue modeling—with a clear methodological contribution (CVAE and knowledge-guided kgCVAE) that aims to improve discourse-level diversity and interpretability, making it highly relevant to the venue. The use of standard datasets (Switchboard), appropriate baselines (encoder–decoder, VAE-LM), and sensible training/evaluation practices (KL annealing, BoW loss, BLEU/perplexity/embedding-based metrics) matches community expectations. From my dialogue-researcher perspective, the work speaks directly to the conference audience interested in latent-variable models, controllable generation, and discourse-aware dialogue systems, and is positioned and evaluated in a way that merits top-tier consideration.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural conversational modeling—with a methodological contribution (CVAE/kgCVAE) and practical training fixes (KL annealing, bag-of-words loss) that are directly relevant to top-tier venues. It evaluates on standard data (Switchboard, PTB), compares to reasonable baselines (encoder–decoder, VAE-LM), reports hyperparameters, and uses established metrics (BLEU, embedding-based diversity, KL/perplexity), matching community expectations. The presentation (thorough related work, clear stated limitations) and focus on discourse-level diversity target the right audience and standards for a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML research topic—neural dialogue modeling—with a clear methodological contribution (CVAE and knowledge-guided kgCVAE) that aims to improve discourse-level diversity and interpretability, making it highly relevant to the venue. The use of standard datasets (Switchboard), appropriate baselines (encoder–decoder, VAE-LM), and sensible training/evaluation practices (KL annealing, BoW loss, BLEU/perplexity/embedding-based metrics) matches community expectations. From my dialogue-researcher perspective, the work speaks directly to the conference audience interested in latent-variable models, controllable generation, and discourse-aware dialogue systems, and is positioned and evaluated in a way that merits top-tier consideration.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—modeling discourse-level diversity in neural dialog—which is central to improving conversational agents, and it proposes a clear technical solution (CVAE and kgCVAE) that yields more diverse, discourse-aware responses on Switchboard and produces interpretable latent structure. The introduction of knowledge-guided conditioning and pragmatic training fixes (bag-of-words loss, KL annealing) are methodological contributions that other researchers can adopt to mitigate the vanishing latent variable issue, so the results are likely to change practice for dialog generation research. While the approach requires careful optimization and substantial data (a noted limitation), the ideas open concrete avenues for discourse-conditioned generation and control, making the work of strong, broad interest rather than niche. | B(4): This paper tackles an important and recognized problem—modeling discourse-level diversity in neural dialog—by framing it through a CVAE and a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that ties latent structure to linguistic features (y), which is valuable for interpretability and control. The proposed training fixes (bag-of-words loss, KL annealing) and the empirical evidence of interpretable latent factors correlated with dialog acts on Switchboard are likely to change how researchers approach latent-variable dialog models and reduce a key optimization barrier. While not necessarily transformative across all of NLP, the work opens meaningful new directions for controllable, discourse-aware generation and should be adopted and extended by researchers building dialogue systems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—modeling discourse-level diversity in neural dialog—which is central to improving conversational agents, and it proposes a clear technical solution (CVAE and kgCVAE) that yields more diverse, discourse-aware responses on Switchboard and produces interpretable latent structure. The introduction of knowledge-guided conditioning and pragmatic training fixes (bag-of-words loss, KL annealing) are methodological contributions that other researchers can adopt to mitigate the vanishing latent variable issue, so the results are likely to change practice for dialog generation research. While the approach requires careful optimization and substantial data (a noted limitation), the ideas open concrete avenues for discourse-conditioned generation and control, making the work of strong, broad interest rather than niche.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important and recognized problem—modeling discourse-level diversity in neural dialog—by framing it through a CVAE and a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that ties latent structure to linguistic features (y), which is valuable for interpretability and control. The proposed training fixes (bag-of-words loss, KL annealing) and the empirical evidence of interpretable latent factors correlated with dialog acts on Switchboard are likely to change how researchers approach latent-variable dialog models and reduce a key optimization barrier. While not necessarily transformative across all of NLP, the work opens meaningful new directions for controllable, discourse-aware generation and should be adopted and extended by researchers building dialogue systems.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized with a clear flow from motivation through method to experiments, and its main contributions (CVAE and kgCVAE, training fixes) are stated early. Notation and methodological details (reparameterization, conditioning on c, incorporation of y) are presented sufficiently for reproducibility, and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is good. However, the summary does not confirm the effectiveness or presence of illustrative figures/tables or released code, so there may be minor presentation gaps for readers seeking visual summaries or runnable artifacts. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions (CVAE, kgCVAE, and training improvements like bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) are stated early and remain clear throughout. Notation appears consistent and definitions (z, c, y, recognition/decoder components) are described, and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and informative, and the writing quality is generally good, though a few sections could more tightly connect the interpretability claims to the presented qualitative examples to improve readability for discourse-focused readers.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well organized with a clear flow from motivation through method to experiments, and its main contributions (CVAE and kgCVAE, training fixes) are stated early. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against sensible baselines: a standard encoder–decoder dialog model (Serban et al.-style) and VAE/LSTM language-model baselines (Bowman et al.) using the same Switchboard data and a variety of metrics (BLEU, bag-of-word cosine, perplexity, KL). The authors also analyze optimization issues and report hyperparameters, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the evaluation appears to omit some other competitive or diversity-focused dialog baselines (e.g., MMI-style objective, retrieval-augmented models, or recent alternative diversity-promoting techniques), so while comparisons are good and mostly fair, a few relevant baselines are missing. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (a standard encoder–decoder dialog model similar to Serban et al., 2016a, and VAE/VAE-LM baselines as in Bowman et al., 2015) using the same SWITCHBOARD data and shared metrics (BLEU, bag-of-word cosine, perplexity, variational lower bound). The authors explicitly discuss training challenges and limitations and analyze how their kgCVAE and training tricks address differences versus baselines, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions remain: the summary does not indicate comparison to some recent hierarchical/latent dialog variants (e.g., later VHRED-style models), so while comparisons are solid and fair, they are not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible baselines: a standard encoder–decoder dialog model (Serban et al.-style) and VAE/LSTM language-model baselines (Bowman et al.) using the same Switchboard data and a variety of metrics (BLEU, bag-of-word cosine, perplexity, KL). The authors also analyze optimization issues and report hyperparameters, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the evaluation appears to omit some other competitive or diversity-focused dialog baselines (e.g., MMI-style objective, retrieval-augmented models, or recent alternative diversity-promoting techniques), so while comparisons are good and mostly fair, a few relevant baselines are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (a standard encoder–decoder dialog model similar to Serban et al., 2016a, and VAE/VAE-LM baselines as in Bowman et al., 2015) using the same SWITCHBOARD data and shared metrics (BLEU, bag-of-word cosine, perplexity, variational lower bound). The authors explicitly discuss training challenges and limitations and analyze how their kgCVAE and training tricks address differences versus baselines, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions remain: the summary does not indicate comparison to some recent hierarchical/latent dialog variants (e.g., later VHRED-style models), so while comparisons are solid and fair, they are not fully comprehensive.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper provides solid experimental evidence: it evaluates on Switchboard and PTB, compares to encoder–decoder and VAE baselines, reports BLEU/diversity/KL/perplexity, and explicitly addresses optimization pathologies (KL annealing and BoW loss). Related work is covered and limitations (training difficulty, vanishing latent) are discussed. However, it is unclear whether thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses of the proposed components (kgCVAE vs CVAE, BoW loss, annealing schedules) and multiple runs/statistical variance were fully reported, and code is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility despite hyperparameter reporting. | B(4): The experiments are broadly appropriate (Switchboard comparisons to an encoder–decoder baseline and VAE LM, metrics including BLEU, BOW cosine, variational bound) and the paper evaluates training fixes (KL annealing, bag-of-words loss) that address the vanishing latent problem, supporting the core claims. Related work is reported thorough and limitations (optimization difficulty, data requirements) are acknowledged; hyperparameters are given. However, the summary does not indicate human evaluation of dialog quality, explicit ablation isolating each proposed component (e.g., kgCVAE vs. CVAE with/without BoW loss), or public code, so reproducibility and some empirical sensitivity analyses remain only partially demonstrated.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper provides solid experimental evidence: it evaluates on Switchboard and PTB, compares to encoder–decoder and VAE baselines, reports BLEU/diversity/KL/perplexity, and explicitly addresses optimization pathologies (KL annealing and BoW loss). Related work is covered and limitations (training difficulty, vanishing latent) are discussed. However, it is unclear whether thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses of the proposed components (kgCVAE vs CVAE, BoW loss, annealing schedules) and multiple runs/statistical variance were fully reported, and code is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility despite hyperparameter reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broadly appropriate (Switchboard comparisons to an encoder–decoder baseline and VAE LM, metrics including BLEU, BOW cosine, variational bound) and the paper evaluates training fixes (KL annealing, bag-of-words loss) that address the vanishing latent problem, supporting the core claims. Related work is reported thorough and limitations (optimization difficulty, data requirements) are acknowledged; hyperparameters are given. However, the summary does not indicate human evaluation of dialog quality, explicit ablation isolating each proposed component (e.g., kgCVAE vs. CVAE with/without BoW loss), or public code, so reproducibility and some empirical sensitivity analyses remain only partially demonstrated.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its claims and supports them with experiments on Switchboard comparing CVAE/kgCVAE to encoder-decoder and VAE baselines, and reports relevant metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine, KL, perplexity). Mathematical setup (CVAE, reparameterization) and training remedies (KL annealing, bag-of-words loss) are standard and appear correctly applied. However, the summary does not mention released code, multiple random runs, variance estimates, or statistical significance testing, which weakens reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims. Overall the methodology is sound and conclusions are plausible, but minor gaps in experimental rigor and reproducibility prevent a perfect score. | B(4): The paper's main claims about CVAE/kgCVAE architectures and the need for KL annealing/BOW loss are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on Switchboard and LM comparisons (they report BLEU, bag-of-word cosine, variational lower bound and perplexity). The methodological setup includes appropriate baselines (encoder–decoder, VAE-LM) and standard optimization tricks; mathematical usage of CVAE and reparameterization is standard and thus likely correct. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, human evaluation for dialog quality, or released code, leaving some experimental rigor and reproducibility aspects under-supported. Overall the technical evidence is strong but a few experimental controls and evaluation modalities seem missing, so I rate it as a minor-issue paper.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its claims and supports them with experiments on Switchboard comparing CVAE/kgCVAE to encoder-decoder and VAE baselines, and reports relevant metrics (BLEU, embedding cosine, KL, perplexity). Mathematical setup (CVAE, reparameterization) and training remedies (KL annealing, bag-of-words loss) are standard and appear correctly applied. However, the summary does not mention released code, multiple random runs, variance estimates, or statistical significance testing, which weakens reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims. Overall the methodology is sound and conclusions are plausible, but minor gaps in experimental rigor and reproducibility prevent a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims about CVAE/kgCVAE architectures and the need for KL annealing/BOW loss are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on Switchboard and LM comparisons (they report BLEU, bag-of-word cosine, variational lower bound and perplexity). The methodological setup includes appropriate baselines (encoder–decoder, VAE-LM) and standard optimization tricks; mathematical usage of CVAE and reparameterization is standard and thus likely correct. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, human evaluation for dialog quality, or released code, leaving some experimental rigor and reproducibility aspects under-supported. Overall the technical evidence is strong but a few experimental controls and evaluation modalities seem missing, so I rate it as a minor-issue paper.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper brings a notable and focused novelty by applying conditional VAEs to model discourse-level diversity in neural dialog and by proposing a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions on explicit linguistic/discourse features y—this goes beyond prior encoder–decoder and VAE language modeling work cited (Serban et al., Bowman et al.). The addition of targeted training strategies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) addresses a known optimization failure mode in a practical way, making the techniques a non-trivial extension rather than a simple recombination of existing methods. From the summary, the contributions are clearly differentiated and would plausibly inform experts working on dialog generation, though they build on established VAE foundations rather than introducing entirely new theoretical machinery. | B(4): From the provided summary, the paper advances the field by applying CVAEs specifically to capture discourse-level diversity in dialog and by proposing the kgCVAE that conditions on and predicts explicit linguistic/discourse features y — a clear extension beyond standard VAE/dialog models. The optimization remedies (KL annealing and bag-of-words loss) are useful but mostly incremental relative to existing VAE training tricks; the primary novelty rests in framing and integrating discourse supervision into the latent structure. As a dialogue researcher, I find the explicit y-conditioning and simultaneous label prediction a notable and interpretable extension that would inform experts, even if some training components reuse known techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper brings a notable and focused novelty by applying conditional VAEs to model discourse-level diversity in neural dialog and by proposing a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions on explicit linguistic/discourse features y—this goes beyond prior encoder–decoder and VAE language modeling work cited (Serban et al., Bowman et al.). The addition of targeted training strategies (bag-of-words loss and KL annealing) addresses a known optimization failure mode in a practical way, making the techniques a non-trivial extension rather than a simple recombination of existing methods. From the summary, the contributions are clearly differentiated and would plausibly inform experts working on dialog generation, though they build on established VAE foundations rather than introducing entirely new theoretical machinery.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary, the paper advances the field by applying CVAEs specifically to capture discourse-level diversity in dialog and by proposing the kgCVAE that conditions on and predicts explicit linguistic/discourse features y — a clear extension beyond standard VAE/dialog models. The optimization remedies (KL annealing and bag-of-words loss) are useful but mostly incremental relative to existing VAE training tricks; the primary novelty rests in framing and integrating discourse supervision into the latent structure. As a dialogue researcher, I find the explicit y-conditioning and simultaneous label prediction a notable and interpretable extension that would inform experts, even if some training components reuse known techniques.

main_claims: Propose a novel neural dialog model based on Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAE) that introduces a latent variable to capture discourse-level diversity in responses.

Introduce a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions the model on explicitly extracted linguistic/discourse features (y), enabling better encoding of discourse information and simultaneous prediction of high-level labels (e.g., dialog acts).

Develop training improvements (including a bag-of-word loss and KL annealing) to address optimization challenges of CVAE for natural language generation, notably the vanishing latent variable problem.

Demonstrate that the proposed models generate significantly more diverse and discourse-aware responses than baseline encoder-decoder approaches and exhibit interpretable latent structure correlated with dialog acts and response length.
_summary.methodology: Use a conditional variational autoencoder where a latent variable z models discourse-level variations conditioned on dialog context c; extend this with kgCVAE by incorporating linguistic features y that are predicted from z and c and fed to the decoder; train with KL annealing and a bag-of-words loss and use standard neural encoders/decoders (BRNN/GRU) and the reparameterization trick.
datasets: Penn Treebank (for VAE language modeling comparison)
baselines: Encoder-decoder neural dialog model without latent variables (similar to Serban et al., 2016a)
Standard VAE/VAE-LM baseline (as in Bowman et al., 2015) for LM comparison
limitations: Training CVAE is a challenging optimization problem and often requires large amounts of data.
CVAE with RNN decoders suffers from the vanishing latent variable problem (recognizer ignored by decoder) if not addressed.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with extensive experience building and evaluating neural dialogue systems, focusing on rigorous experimental methodology and reproducible research practices.
traits: inspects experimental design closely: expects clear baselines, ablation studies, and controlled comparisons
values reproducibility: looks for hyperparameters, training details, dataset splits, and releaseable code or seeds
checks statistical and empirical rigor: notices variance, significance testing, and multiple runs for stochastic models
focuses on optimization and implementation issues: attentive to training tricks (e.g., KL annealing), failure modes (vanishing latent), and computational cost
evaluates evaluation methodology: examines automatic metrics, human evaluation protocols, and whether claims are supported by measured results
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Systematic and practical: I judge whether experiments and reported results are sufficient, reproducible, and fairly compared to support the paper's empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Dialogue Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor working on dialogue modeling and conversational AI, with a track record of work on latent-variable models and discourse-level representations.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning: notices how contributions differ from prior CVAE and latent-dialog work and whether the idea meaningfully advances the field
values interpretability and linguistic grounding: looks for evidence that latent variables capture discourse phenomena and for connections to dialog act theory or task utility
cares about broader impact and applicability: evaluates whether the approach improves downstream dialog quality, controllability, or usability in real systems
examines evaluation realism: attentive to whether datasets, metrics, and human studies reflect real conversational needs and diversity notions
notices scalability and generalization: considers whether the method and learned signals (y) transfer across domains and languages
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual and impact-oriented: I emphasize novelty relative to prior work, interpretability of learned representations, and whether the approach meaningfully advances dialogue modeling practice.
